selected_patch: If the question requests the release date of an ordinal work (detect ordinals like 1st/11th/eleventh) of a named music group or other creative group described with a superlative (e.g. "highest-selling"), and the current query lacks discography/release context, construct a compact query by: KEEP the group's name and the superlative descriptor if present; RESTORE relation tokens: "discography" OR "nth" OR "release date" OR "released on"; RETAIN nearby timeline context (years) if present in the input; DROP unrelated entity mentions that collapse candidate sets; STYLE as a terse keywords string: "<GroupName> discography 11th release released on". Only apply when the query does not already include explicit discography/release-date tokens.

Conditional rule: when the question contains a containment/location relation (detect tokens: "which country", "what country", "located in", "part of", "terminus", "ends at", "where" + place-type) but the candidate hop2 query lacks an explicit relation token, append one short relation cue that matches the question (examples: "which country", "located in", "terminus at"). Reversible edit-script template to apply only when a relation is missing: KEEP: primary anchor entity token(s); RESTORE: one compact relation cue (e.g. "which country" / "located in" / "terminus at"); RETAIN: any alternate short place names already present; DROP: higher-level topical tokens only if they conflict with the relation; STYLE: keep query compact and keyword-like. Only trigger if the input query does not already contain a relation cue.

When the question requests a "last stationed/last assignment/last station" for a military/unit or similar organizational unit: 1) KEEP the exact unit name anchor as provided. 2) RESTORE concise assignment/station tokens: append short BM25 cues such as "last stationed" OR "stationed at" OR "last assigned" and common facility abbreviations (AFB, AFB variants) or 'Air Force Base' spelled short. 3) RETAIN unit-lineage or sector tokens only if the input already includes them (so you preserve candidate set). 4) DROP long provenance/lineage narrative fragments that bring back other unrelated units. STYLE: compact keyword query like: <UnitName> "last stationed" "stationed at" AFB OR "last assigned". Only apply if the input query lacks explicit "last"/"stationed"/"assigned" tokens.

If the question asks for a 'type of' or a name derived from another term (detect tokens: "type", "named after", "term", "name derived", "called"), then when composing the hop2 query: emit a compact edit-script over the current query: KEEP: existing event/entity anchors (proper names, dates, locations). RESTORE: a short etymology/term-origin cue (append keywords: term origin etymology "name derived from" called). RETAIN: ambiguous candidate labels (do not expand them into quoted answers). DROP: peripheral actors or crowd words that cause topic drift (e.g., long lists of associated persons) unless they are the direct anchor. STYLE: keep query keyword-like and short (space-separated keywords). Apply only when the question indicates a term/etymology hop.

When the question asks for people/items by name or asks 'which X', 'which MPs', 'names of', or 'list of', append compact list-page cues (e.g. names OR "List of" + subject-type) to preserve the candidate set. Reversible edit-script: KEEP: subject/topic anchor; RESTORE: list-page cue ('List of' OR names token for the subject); RETAIN: ambiguous qualifiers if multiple list targets possible; DROP: single-item entity tokens that collapse the candidate set; STYLE: keep query compact and focused on list/title phrases. Only apply when the input query does not already include a list-page or names cue.

If the question pattern is "What is the type of <X> that was used by <Y>" or contains both a subject (user's entity) and an object described as 'used'/'used by', then: KEEP the subject name and any explicit titled item (composition title) from the hop1 input; RESTORE compact usage/credit tokens such as used_in used_by credited arrangement version credited_as recorded_as liner_notes credit (as single keywords); RETAIN multiple candidate item titles only if they are explicitly present in the input; DROP unrelated genre or artist names that were not asked about; STYLE: produce a short keyword query combining the subject, the item title (if present), and 2–3 usage/credit tokens. Only activate when the question requests a 'type' or 'kind' of an item that was 'used' by the subject.

When the question asks what X and Y 'have in common' (contains phrasings like have in common / both / share / same culture / same nationality), then KEEP both entity names exactly as in the question. RESTORE a small relation-token set (culture | nationality | origin | 'culture of') and conjunctively require it with both anchors. RETAIN short context tags (e.g. 'film' or 'city') only if the question mentions them explicitly; DROP plot/genre words that cause topic drift. STYLE: emit a compact conjunctive keyword query: "X" "Y" culture nationality origin. Apply only for shared-attribute questions and only when the original query lacks an explicit culture/nationality relation token.

Conditional rewrite rule (bridge-recovery): when the question asks a second-hop relation (keywords: where, which country, home, hosts, plays most of its home, how many, who starred, appeared in, etc.) and the hop1 anchor is a named entity from the input, apply a reversible edit-script to the hop2 query: 
- KEEP: preserve the exact anchor tokens (the same casing/spacing as provided) as contiguous keywords. 
- RESTORE: add one short relation cue token sequence that names the missing bridge relation (choose from a small set of compact cues like: "national team", "home stadium", "located in", "population", "cast" or "appeared in"), but add only the single most relevant cue inferred from the question intent. 
- RETAIN: if the input contains an explicit candidate set (alternate names/disambiguators), keep those tokens literally to avoid over-broadening. 
- DROP: drop short ambiguous substrings from the anchor that are common collisions (e.g., short place-name fragments or partial tokens) if they were not present as the exact anchor phrase in the input. 
- STYLE: produce a compact keyword query: [ANCHOR] + [RELATION_CUE] + optional minimal disambiguator (profession/label/year) only when the input provided it. 
Only trigger this rule when hop1 retrieval was noisy or incomplete (many near-miss titles) or when the question explicitly requests the bridge relation; otherwise do not alter the original keyword set.

If a query contains many high-frequency auxiliary or comparative terms (e.g., duration/compare/versus or multiple unrelated place names) that dilute retrieval focus, apply a conservative edit: KEEP the primary object/ride/item or named anchor from the question; DROP peripheral comparison targets and noisy venue/location tokens unless the question explicitly asks for the comparison metric; RESTORE a concise location cue only when the question asks 'located in' or 'hosted at' (use tokens located OR location OR hosted); RETAIN any small necessary comparator (one other anchor) if the question's semantics require it; STYLE: keep the query short and keyword-like to favor BM25 exact-title matches.

If the input query contains a multi-word or quoted title (heuristic: >1 title-case tokens or quotes) and the question is asking for a person/entity associated with that title (question words include: who, which person, who sang/performed/starring/co‑star), then append a compact bridge cue rather than extra collaborators. Concretely: KEEP the original title tokens; RESTORE by appending one of the short bridge tokens: performed by | credited to | creator | starring (choose the single best match that fits the question); RETAIN any explicit candidate list supplied by the user (OR or quoted names); DROP unrelated collaborator names only when the bridge cue is appended. Always produce a terse, keyword-only final query (no sentences). Only apply when the query lacks an explicit performer/creator cue already. This edit is reversible by re-inserting dropped collaborator tokens if the original query explicitly listed them as candidates.
